title: write /dev/stderr: input/output error while using a podman socket in the init.d system
body:
### Issue Description

**Background**
Podman is running as a service on a Linux industrial PC (BPC 9102S) from Phoenix Contact.
The Linux firmware version in use employs init.d for system initialization.
The Podman remote socket is started as an init.d service in the project.

**Issue**
The Podman socket appears to be in an undefined state. The issue occurs after an indeterminate runtime (ranging from a few hours to several days) and is reproducible from that point until the socket and the socket are restarted.

Further tests have shown that updating Podman does not improve the situation.
In a systemd-based operating system, the issue cannot be reproduced.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

The issue is just reproducable with the whole customer application applied on the Phoenix Contact device. 
There is one setup to reproduce the issue at Phoenix Contact.


### Describe the results you received

When commands are executed via the Podman socket, the following error is returned:
`write /dev/stderr: input/output error`

Stacktrace of the socket process:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1ee459a8-60bb-4e54-99d6-260715e678e5)

### Describe the results you expected

It is expected that the error `write /dev/stderr: input/output error` will not occur in the init.d system.

### Podman in a container

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: We only support the latest version upstream, so you should first try to reproduce this on the latest version so we know this isn't already fixed. Without a solid reproducer there isn't really much we can do otherwise.

Thank you for your response.
Podman is included in the firmware version of the Phoenix Contact device, so we had no chance to update the version.

Nevertheless, we confirmerd that there was a change between Systemd and SysVinit, but we found a solution for us.
Nothing to do for you!
